entries.position_summary: The United States, in cooperation with the IAEA, is launching an International School on Peaceful Uses to train developing countries in responsible civilian nuclear applications. This initiative aims to build capacity across sectors like health, agriculture, water, environment, waste management, and industry, fostering global partnerships and sustainable development. It underscores the U.S. commitment to promoting peaceful nuclear technologies through education and collaboration.
entries.quote: The United States, in cooperation with the IAEA, has also developed an International School on Peaceful Uses of Nuclear Applications, an educational and collaborative initiative for developing countries to advance understanding and applications of nuclear technologies for peaceful purposes, including how nuclear technology can be responsibly used for socio-economic development. The School will address various applications for peaceful uses, including radiopharmaceuticals, agriculture and food security, water management and isotope hydrology, environmental monitoring and waste management, and industrial applications and non- destructive testing. The pilot program will take place this fall in the United States. The School is not only a platform for knowledge dissemination but also a catalyst for fostering global partnerships and advancing scientific and technological progress. This initiative underscores the U.S. commitment to promoting the peaceful uses of nuclear technology, which reinforces international cooperation for sustainable development.

entries.position_summary: The country supports preserving and expanding attention to cross-cutting issues—transparency/reporting, education and capacity-building, and gender/diversity—within the NPT. It proposes dedicating plenary sessions at Review Conferences and the Preparatory Committee to these topics.
entries.quote: • Preserving and potentially expanding opportunities to address cross-cutting issues, such as transparency, reporting, education and capacity-building, gender and diversity. These could be addressed in dedicated plenary sessions of review conferences and the Preparatory Committee.
